title: Observation of attractor transitions in active magnon-polaritons under microwatt drives
authors: Hao Wu, Qichun Liu, Yuanbin Fan, Yulong Liu, Qing Zhao
abstract: Magnon-polaritons provide a room-temperature platform for investigating nonlinear cavity quantum electrodynamics in the microwave domain, but experimentally observing controlled transitions among distinct nonlinear attractors remains challenging in conventional passive systems, where strong external driving is usually required. Here we report the observation of attractor transitions in an active magnon-polariton formed by a self-oscillating microwave cavity coupled to a yttrium iron garne (YIG) sphere. The feedback loop supplies an internal microwave drive, while Kerr frequency pulling and Suhl-mediated magnon-magnon scattering produce an enhanced effective nonlinearity. Stability analysis using experimentally calibrated parameters reveals a rich fixed-point (FP) landscape with multiple unstable-FP phases and a triple-point region. By tuning gain across these phases, we observe the first experimental evidence of explosive growth of bistability, followed by transitions to multifrequency limit cycles, comb-like/fractal spectra, and broadband chaotic dynamics at microwatt powers. Near a critical point, magnetic-field-triggered switching between nonlinear emission states produces spectral shifts up to 162 times the bare gyromagnetic response. By enabling low-power attractor transitions and attractor-switching-amplified spectral response, active magnon-polaritons open opportunities for nonlinear microwave signal generation, high-precision sensing, and neuromorphic computing.
summary: This paper reports the first experimental observation of complex attractor transitions, including chaos and frequency combs, in an active magnon-polariton system driven by microwatt powers. By using a self-oscillating cavity, the researchers achieved nonlinear control at power levels four orders of magnitude lower than previous passive systems. This work opens new avenues for low-power nonlinear microwave signal generation and neuromorphic computing.
main_problem: The difficulty of experimentally observing and controlling transitions between distinct nonlinear attractors (such as chaos and frequency combs) in conventional passive magnon-polariton systems, which typically require high-power milliwatt-class drives.
main_result: The observation of a full route to chaos and the 'explosive growth of bistability' in an active magnon-polariton system using extremely low microwatt-level microwave drives.
method: The researchers used a self-oscillating microwave cavity coupled to a YIG sphere, employing stability analysis of fixed points and numerical simulations of a van der Pol oscillator model to characterize nonlinear dynamics.
model_or_system: An active magnon-polariton platform consisting of a 1-mm YIG sphere coupled to a microstrip-line cavity with a BJT-based gain element, modeled as a van der Pol oscillator with Kerr nonlinearity and Suhl-mediated scattering.
key_observables: Intra-cavity photon number, frequency spectra (limit cycles, fractals, frequency combs, and broadband chaos), and spectral shifts.
important_parameters: Microwatt-level driving power (-35 to -10 dBm), spin-photon coupling strength (g/2pi = 25 MHz), and effective nonlinearity (K/2pi = 3.2 uHz).
main_assumptions: The system is modeled using a semiclassical mean-field level approach.
figures_summary: Fig 1 compares active vs. passive models and shows simulated phase diagrams; Fig 2 shows the experimental setup and linear dependence; Fig 4 displays experimental frequency offsets, spectral zooms (fractals/chaos), and magnetic-field-dependent Rabi splittings; Fig 5 shows power-dependent stability phase diagrams.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the active vs. passive MP models, describes the experimental setup and BJT-driven architecture, presents stability analysis and phase diagrams, details the experimental observation of power-dependent attractor transitions (from bistability to chaos), and concludes with potential applications in sensing and computing.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a highly controllable, low-power platform for studying driven-dissipative nonlinear dynamics, period-doubling, and chaos, which are central themes in open quantum systems and nonlinear cavity QED.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Models and stability analysis of the active and passive magnon-polaritons (MP). a,b, Schematics of the active and passive MP models where a cavity photon mode a is coupled to a magnon mode m possessing the nonlinearity K. The coupling is characterized by the spin-photon coupling strength g and the damping of the a and m modes is denoted by κ and γ respectively. The difference between the active and passive MP arises from the driving mechanisms. For the active MP, a self-sustained a mode, acting like a van-der Pol (vdP) oscillator, is established via a feedback architecture consisting of the linear gain G and nonlinear gain saturation Γ|a|2 depending on the photon number, while for...

FIG. 2. Experimental setup and microwave characterizations of the magnon and bare cavity modes. a, The half-wavelength microstrip- line cavity is fabricated on a printed circuit board (PCB) and the gain is provided by a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) which is powered by two-port DC voltages (one is ﬁxed at 0.7 V and the other Vc is adjustable). A yttrium iron garnet (YIG) sphere surrounded by a loop antenna is ﬁxed in a sample holder (not shown) above the PCB and movable via a three-axis (x,y and z) translation stage. The whole setup is positioned between a pair of electromagnets supplying horizontal static magnetic ﬁelds. There are three RF ports used for the microwave...

FIG. 3. Onset of attractor transitions under microwatt-level drive. a,b,c,d, Experimental and simulated results of the frequency offset of the vdP oscillator caused by its coupling to the YIG sphere subject to an up-sweeping magnetic ﬁeld. ∆0m = ωm −ω0 d is the magnon-drive (cavity) detuning between the magnetic-ﬁeld-dependent magnon mode frequency and the initial vdP oscillator frequency. The driving power Pd is varied by the gain in the circuit tuned by Vc and determined according to the vdP oscillator output power. The adjustment of Pd in the simulation is realized by tuning the gain factor G. e, Zoom-in of the sidebands observed in the dashed box of c.

FIG. 4. Route to chaotic active MP. a,b,e,g, Experimental results of the frequency offset of the vdP oscillator coupled to the YIG sphere subject to a up-sweeping magnetic ﬁeld under the driving powers beyond 1 µW (-30 dBm). c,d, Zoom-in of the rich nonlinear phenomena observed in the dashed box of b where the four nonlinear regimes show (i) multifrequency limit cycles, (ii) fractals, (iii) frequency-comb-like structure and (iv) chaos. f, Switching of the vdP output spectrum from the multimode to single-mode emission observed in e. The signiﬁcant peak shifts occur within a change of the magnetic ﬁeld less than 0.2 G. h, Zoom-in of the magnetic-ﬁeld-dependent Rabi splittings of the vdP...

FIG. 5. Power-dependent stability phase diagram of the active MP revealing its power efﬁciency in achieving nonlinear attractor transitions. a, Dependence of the critical detuning in real time ∆m = ∆0m −Ωmeasured at different driving powers (left panel). Simulated phase diagrams of the stable and unstable FPs in ∆m −G space of the active MP (right panel). The parameters used for generating the phase diagrams are obtained from the simulation shown in Figs. 3b and 3d that reproduce the experimental results. Spintronics.note: The paper directly studies the physics of magnon-polaritons and nonlinear dynamics in a YIG-cavity system, which is a central theme of the researcher's spintronics work.
